policy.title: RBI Issues Final Guidelines on Climate-Related Financial Risk Management for Scheduled Commercial Banks
policy.description: RBI mandates all scheduled commercial banks to integrate climate scenario analysis into ICAAP by March 2027, with specific requirements for physical risk assessment of loan portfolios exceeding ₹100 crore exposure to climate-sensitive sectors. Banks must report climate-adjusted PD estimates for agriculture, real estate, and infrastructure portfolios.

policy.title: SEBI Expands BRSR Core to Top 500 Listed Companies with Enhanced Climate Metrics
policy.description: SEBI extends mandatory BRSR Core reporting to top 500 companies by market capitalization effective FY2026-27, adding Scope 3 emissions disclosure for high-impact sectors and water stress metrics. Third-party assurance now required for all quantitative climate indicators.
policy.source: Securities and Exchange Board of India

policy.title: IRDAI Releases Exposure Draft on Index-Based Insurance Products for Climate Perils
policy.description: IRDAI proposes framework allowing insurers to file parametric products for flood, cyclone, and heat stress with streamlined approval process. Draft specifies acceptable trigger indices including IMD rainfall data, wind speed thresholds, and satellite-derived flood extent.
policy.source: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India

policy.title: BCBS Publishes Pillar 3 Climate Risk Disclosure Framework for Adoption by 2028
policy.description: Basel Committee finalizes standardized disclosure templates for banks' climate-related financial risks, including mandatory reporting of financed emissions, physical risk concentrations by geography, and transition risk metrics. Implementation timeline set for January 2028 with phased approach for emerging market banks.

policy.title: ISSB Releases Jurisdiction Guide for Emerging Markets Adopting IFRS S1 and S2
policy.description: IFRS Foundation publishes implementation guidance specifically for emerging market regulators, including proportionality provisions and a three-year transition pathway. India referenced as key jurisdiction with SEBI-ISSB alignment expected by 2027.

policy.title: NGFS Releases Phase V Climate Scenarios with Enhanced Physical Risk Granularity for Asia
policy.description: Updated scenarios include 10km resolution physical hazard projections for South and Southeast Asia, with specific modules for monsoon variability, compound flooding, and agricultural heat stress. New chronic risk indicators added for infrastructure lending assessments.
policy.source: Network for Greening the Financial System

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence Partners with HDFC Bank for Physical Risk Assessment Platform
competitors.description: Jupiter announces enterprise agreement with HDFC Bank to provide ClimateScore Financial for commercial real estate and infrastructure loan portfolios. Partnership includes customization for Indian monsoon and cyclone perils with 90-meter resolution.

competitors.title: Sprih Raises $12M Series A to Expand Climate Risk Modules for Indian Banks
competitors.description: Bengaluru-based Sprih closes Series A led by Nexus Venture Partners to build physical risk scoring for MSME lending. Company claims 15 NBFC clients and plans to add parametric insurance analytics by Q3 2026.

competitors.title: Moody's Launches India-Specific Physical Risk Scores for Banking Sector
competitors.description: Moody's releases localized physical risk dataset covering 650 Indian districts with hazard scores for flood, cyclone, drought, and heat stress. Product integrates with Moody's credit rating workflows and targets Indian bank compliance with RBI climate guidelines.

competitors.title: XDI Expands APAC Coverage with Asset-Level Physical Risk Data for South Asia
competitors.description: Australian climate analytics firm XDI releases Gross Asset Value at Risk metrics for 50 million assets across India, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. Partners with Singapore-based banks for real estate portfolio stress testing.

competitors.title: Watershed Partners with Yes Bank for Financed Emissions Platform
competitors.description: US-based carbon accounting platform Watershed announces partnership with Yes Bank to measure and report Scope 3 financed emissions across commercial lending portfolio. Deal signals increased competition in Indian FI sustainability software market.

competitors.title: GIST Advisory Launches Natural Capital Risk Module for Agricultural Lending
competitors.description: GIST releases biodiversity and ecosystem services risk assessment tool targeting Indian agricultural finance. Product maps loan portfolios against water stress, soil degradation, and pollinator dependency using proprietary district-level datasets.

research.title: Climate Risk and Financial Stability in Emerging Markets: Evidence from South Asia
research.description: BIS study finds South Asian banks face potential 8-15% increase in NPAs under severe physical risk scenarios by 2035, with agricultural and MSME portfolios most exposed. Recommends immediate integration of forward-looking climate metrics into provisioning models.

research.title: Scaling Parametric Insurance in Climate-Vulnerable Regions: Product Design and Distribution
research.description: Report identifies India as highest-potential market for parametric crop and flood insurance, estimating addressable market of $2.8 billion by 2030. Highlights bank-assurer partnerships as critical distribution channel for reaching MSME and agricultural segments.

research.title: Physical Climate Risk Assessment in Credit Portfolios: Methodological Framework
research.description: UNEPFI publishes standardized methodology for banks to translate physical hazard data into PD and LGD adjustments. Framework tested with 12 pilot banks globally, including two Indian institutions, showing 2-4% LGD variance for flood-exposed commercial real estate.

research.title: ESG Data Gaps in Emerging Markets: Challenges and Solutions for Financial Institutions
research.description: IFC analysis reveals 65% of listed companies in emerging markets lack adequate climate disclosure, with India performing better than regional peers at 48% coverage. Recommends use of alternative data sources including satellite imagery and supply chain proxies.

research.title: Monsoon Variability and Agricultural Credit Risk in India
research.description: CRISIL estimates monsoon variability could increase agricultural loan defaults by 12-18% in vulnerable districts under 2°C warming scenario. Identifies 127 high-risk districts where physical risk adjustments should be prioritized in credit underwriting.

research.title: Index-Based Flood Insurance for Urban India: Trigger Design and Basis Risk Analysis
research.description: World Bank technical paper proposes satellite-based flood extent triggers for parametric products in Indian metros, finding basis risk below 15% for Chennai, Mumbai, and Kolkata when using Sentinel-1 SAR data. Recommends hybrid triggers combining rainfall and inundation indices.

ourRead.headline: RBI's March guidelines create a 12-month sprint for Indian banks to operationalize climate-adjusted credit risk—StepChange must position as the implementation partner, not just a data vendor.
body: This month's signals converge on a single theme: the regulatory-to-implementation gap is closing fast. RBI's final climate risk guidelines, SEBI's expanded BRSR Core, and IRDAI's parametric framework are no longer consultation papers—they are compliance mandates with hard deadlines. Indian financial institutions now face simultaneous pressure to quantify physical risk in lending, report financed emissions, and design new insurance products. The institutions that moved early are already selecting vendors; the laggards are scrambling.

The RBI guidelines (policy-1) are the most consequential development for StepChange's core business. The requirement for climate-adjusted PD estimates in agriculture, real estate, and infrastructure portfolios by March 2027 creates immediate demand for exactly what we build. Critically, the ₹100 crore threshold means mid-sized NBFCs—our sweet spot—are now in scope. We should prepare compliance-ready PD/LGD modules that map directly to RBI's specified sectors and reporting templates. First-mover advantage in regulatory alignment will be decisive.

The competitor landscape is getting crowded and sophisticated. Jupiter's HDFC partnership (comp-1) and Moody's India-specific physical risk scores (comp-3) signal that global players are no longer treating India as an afterthought. Meanwhile, Sprih's Series A (comp-2) shows domestic competitors building climate risk for MSME lending—our target market. StepChange's differentiation must come from deeper India localization (monsoon, cyclone, district-level granularity) and integrated parametric insurance capabilities that neither Jupiter nor Sprih currently offer. We cannot compete on brand; we must compete on regulatory fit and local hazard expertise.

The Swiss Re parametric insurance report (research-2) validates our second strategic bet with hard numbers: $2.8 billion addressable market by 2030, with bank-assurer partnerships as the critical channel. Combined with the World Bank's trigger design work (research-6) showing sub-15% basis risk for satellite-based flood indices, we have the technical foundation to help banks design credible parametric products. The IRDAI draft framework (policy-3) explicitly accepts IMD and satellite data as trigger indices—this is our signal to package a turnkey parametric product design toolkit for bank partners.

One thing to watch: Watershed's Yes Bank partnership (comp-5) suggests global ESG platforms are entering India through financed emissions, not physical risk. This could commoditize Scope 3 reporting before we've built that capability. One action to take: Schedule deep-dive workshops with 3-5 mid-sized NBFCs this quarter specifically on RBI compliance pathways—we need to convert the regulatory pressure into signed pilots before Jupiter and Moody's lock up the large bank segment.
